summarization.a: Resistivity versus temperature (1.7 to 6 K) showing superconducting transitions for three TiN films. The 60 nm ALD film (blue) has Tc ≈ 3.22 K and resistivity ~222 μΩcm. The ALE-treated 50 nm film (red) and 50 nm ALD reference (black) show nearly identical Tc ≈ 3.1 K, demonstrating that ALE does not degrade superconducting properties beyond the expected thickness dependence.

a.question: How does the superconducting critical temperature of the ALE-treated film compare to the ALD reference film of the same thickness?
a.answer: The ALE-treated 50 nm film has Tc ≈ 3.13 K, which is nearly identical to the 50 nm ALD reference film at Tc ≈ 3.11 K. This negligible difference demonstrates that the ALE process does not introduce damage that would degrade superconducting properties. The small Tc reduction from the 60 nm film (Tc ≈ 3.22 K) is consistent with expected thickness-dependent behavior.

a.question: What does this figure demonstrate about the damage characteristics of the ALE process?
a.answer: The figure demonstrates that the plasma-thermal ALE process is low-damage. Conventional etching methods often degrade superconducting properties through subsurface damage or contamination. Here, the ALE-treated film maintains the same Tc as an as-deposited film of equal thickness, confirming that ALE removes material without harming the remaining film quality.

a.question: Why does the ALE-treated film show lower resistivity at 6 K than the 50 nm ALD film?
a.answer: The ALE-treated film has lower resistivity (~201 μΩcm) compared to the 50 nm ALD film (~227 μΩcm) due to reduced native oxide after etching. The authors hypothesize that fluorine incorporation during ALE may also act as a diffusion barrier, slowing reoxidation when exposed to atmosphere.

a.question: What measurement technique was used to obtain this data?
a.answer: Four-point resistivity measurements using a Quantum Design DynaCool Physical Property Measurement System (PPMS) from 6 K down to 1.7 K.
